report_name: Microsoft Uncovers Sophisticated Phishing Campaign Hitting 35,000 Users Globally

attack_patterns.name: Spearphishing Link
attack_patterns.description: The campaign used links inside PDF attachments to direct users to a malicious adversary-in-the-middle (AiTM) phishing site.

attack_patterns.name: Spearphishing Attachment
attack_patterns.description: Phishing emails contained PDF attachments with malicious links to bypass some automated email scanners.

attack_patterns.name: Drive-by Compromise
attack_patterns.description: Users were directed to a malicious website that proxied their authentication session to facilitate the theft of session tokens.

attack_patterns.name: Steal Web Session Cookie
attack_patterns.description: The primary goal of the AiTM phishing attack was to steal the user's session token (cookie) after a successful login, allowing the attacker to bypass MFA.

attack_patterns.name: Valid Accounts
attack_patterns.description: Attackers used stolen session tokens to gain access to valid, authenticated user sessions on services like Office 365 and Outlook without needing credentials.

attack_patterns.name: Adversary-in-the-Middle
attack_patterns.description: The campaign utilized an adversary-in-the-middle (AiTM) phishing architecture to proxy user authentication sessions in real-time, capturing session tokens to bypass MFA.

tools.name: Adversary-in-the-Middle (AiTM) phishing kit
tools.description: An AiTM phishing kit was used to proxy user authentication sessions in real-time, allowing attackers to steal valid session cookies and bypass multi-factor authentication.

campaigns.name: Code of Conduct Phishing Campaign (April 2026)
campaigns.description: A large-scale, sophisticated phishing campaign in April 2026 that used 'code of conduct' lures and an AiTM phishing kit to steal session tokens from over 35,000 users, bypassing MFA.

identities.name: Healthcare and Life Sciences Sector
identities.description: The most targeted industry sector in the AiTM phishing campaign, accounting for 19% of victims.

identities.name: Financial Services Sector
identities.description: A primary target of the AiTM phishing campaign, accounting for 18% of victims.

identities.name: Professional Services Sector
identities.description: A key industry targeted in the AiTM phishing campaign, accounting for 11% of victims.

infrastructure.name: AiTM Phishing Infrastructure
infrastructure.description: An adversary-in-the-middle architecture used to host convincing replicas of organizational sign-in pages, proxy authentication, and steal session tokens.

infrastructure.name: Phishing Email Delivery Infrastructure
infrastructure.description: Attacker-controlled domains and a legitimate email delivery service used to send the initial 'code of conduct' phishing emails.

indicators.name: Potential AiTM Activity via Azure AD Failed MFA
indicators.pattern:
title: Potential AiTM Activity via Azure AD Failed MFA
id: 9c5b8e3a-1f9a-4b8e-9c5b-8e3a1f9a8b8e
status: experimental
description: Detects Azure AD sign-in error code 50126, which can indicate a user failed to complete an MFA prompt. This may occur during an adversary-in-the-middle (AiTM) phishing attempt.
references:
  - https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/security/blog/2026/05/04/breaking-the-code-multi-stage-code-of-conduct-phishing-campaign-leads-to-aitm-token-compromise/
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-05-05
tags:
  - attack.t1557
  - attack.credential_access
level: medium
logsource:
  product: azure
  service: signinlogs
detection:
  selection:
    ResultType: '50126'
  condition: selection
indicators.description: Detects Azure AD sign-in error code 50126, which can indicate a user failed to complete an MFA prompt, a potential sign of an AiTM phishing attempt.

indicators.name: Potential AiTM Phishing Kit Server Activity
indicators.pattern:
title: Potential AiTM Phishing Kit Server Activity
id: f4a2b1c3-d4e5-4f6a-b7c8-d9e0f1a2b3c4
status: experimental
description: Detects the use of '--headless' command-line argument, which is often used by AiTM phishing kits on the server side to render login pages. This rule should be deployed on web servers or related infrastructure.
references:
  - https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/security/blog/2026/05/04/breaking-the-code-multi-stage-code-of-conduct-phishing-campaign-leads-to-aitm-token-compromise/
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-05-05
tags:
  - attack.t1557
  - attack.credential_access
level: medium
logsource:
  category: process_creation
  product: linux
detection:
  selection:
    CommandLine|contains: ' --headless'
  condition: selection
indicators.description: Detects the use of a '--headless' command-line argument, a pattern often associated with server-side components of AiTM phishing kits.

relationships.source_name: Potential AiTM Activity via Azure AD Failed MFA
Potential AiTM Phishing Kit Server Activity
